title: Unsealed emails show Pentagon told Anthropic the deal was 'very close' the day it was blacklisted
subtitle: Court filings reveal months of correspondence between Dario Amodei and Undersecretary Emil Michael over guardrails on autonomous weapons and domestic surveillance.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: Court filings unsealed on July 4 reveal months of email exchanges between Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei and Pentagon Undersecretary Emil Michael over the guardrails Anthropic wanted attached to any government use of Claude. The most striking exchange: Michael told Amodei the two sides were 'very close' on contract terms the day after the Pentagon's supply-chain risk designation was finalized — but before Anthropic had been informed. Judge Rita Lin, reviewing the record, called the 'very close' language 'exceedingly difficult to square' with the government's parallel characterization of Anthropic as hostile and posing an intolerable risk.

The dispute is narrower than 'Anthropic vs. the DoD' — it is about which two uses Anthropic refused to allow. Amodei wanted fully autonomous weapons and domestic surveillance off the table. The Pentagon wanted the models to cover 'all lawful uses' — language Amodei read as leaving both uses in. Michael eventually told Anthropic the guardrails were 'just not workable' and offered one final chance to align on 'core principles' before the two sides split. The next day, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth designated Anthropic a supply-chain risk.

This matters for the broader AI-and-defense story because the emails contradict the public narrative on both sides. It was not a policy dispute that could not be resolved — it was a policy dispute the Pentagon told Anthropic in writing was almost resolved, right up to the moment it was blacklisted. That gap between the public and private record is what the appeals-court panel will now weigh, and the outcome will shape how every frontier lab negotiates federal contracts.

A takeaway for learners: 'AI safety' at the policy level is often less about model capability and more about deployment authority — who decides what a model can and cannot be used for after it leaves the developer. If you are studying policy, law, or ethics, this case is a live example of how technical redlines get translated into contract language, and how easily that translation can break. The specific words on the page — 'autonomous,' 'lawful,' 'surveillance' — are load-bearing in a way that can decide billion-dollar contracts.
